subject: English
period_schedule_display:
Period schedule:
  Row 1: 40 minutes × 2 periods = 80 minutes
Total: 2 periods · 1h 20min

periods.activity_title: Helen Keller's Three Days: Reading and Comprehension

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Identify true statements from seven on Keller's essay
Table: what Keller planned for each of the three days

homework.task_brief: Let us discuss (p.32): How does the narrator understand a person's feelings?

phases.description: Teacher briefly introduces Helen Keller — blind and deaf author who accessed the world entirely through touch, smell, and sound — and reads the essay's opening paragraph aloud to establish tone. Students follow in their textbooks.

Students read the full essay silently, then work in pairs to identify the true statements from the seven given (page 31), distinguishing carefully between what Keller actually says and plausible but incorrect options. Teacher facilitates class correction.

Students complete the three-column table of what Keller plans to do on Day 1 (see the people she loves), Day 2 (arise with the dawn, visit museums), and Day 3 (observe a busy city corner) (page 32). Teacher models the Day 1 entry on the board before students complete Days 2 and 3.

Homework: students write a short answer to 'How is the narrator able to understand the feelings of a person?' (page 32) based on Keller's description of knowing friends only through the feel of their faces. Period 11 will cover vocabulary, speaking, and the writing task.

periods.teacher_notes: Keller's essay moves from quiet wonder to passionate advocacy — a tone shift that students in the middle stage may need help tracking. The true/false-style identification task is a good comprehension check before the three-day table, which requires students to synthesise across the full essay. The pre-reading activities (pair recall of a visited place and senses discussion, pages 28–29) are left as self-study since the period is fully used by the comprehension and table tasks.

periods.activity_title: Modal Verbs, Sensory Words, and Speaking About the Senses

tasks_in_class.task_brief: Identify modal verbs and their functions in five sentences
Fill blanks with appropriate modal verbs in seven situations
Speak one minute on the sense you value most

phases.description: Teacher introduces modal verbs using Keller's own sentences ('I should arise with the dawn', 'I must spend the second day') and explains the five functions: possibility, necessity, polite request, ability, moral obligation. Students identify modal verbs and their functions in the five given sentences (page 34).

Students fill the seven blanks with appropriate modal verbs matching the given function labels — permission, obligation, advice, possibility, polite request, moral obligation, suggestion (page 35). Teacher corrects on the board, emphasising why 'ought to' signals moral obligation while 'should' signals advice.

Speaking task: students prepare for one minute, then two or three volunteers speak for one minute on the sense they value most, using the prompt framework from page 36. Class listens and identifies one strong phrase from each speaker.

Teacher closes the chapter by connecting Keller's appeal — 'use your eyes as if tomorrow you might be struck blind' — to the chapter's overarching theme of valuing what we have and learning with gratitude. Sensory-word task (Task I, page 34) and beyond-text tasks (Braille, ISL, IDPD) are flagged as self-study extension for interested students.

periods.teacher_notes: Modal verbs are the hardest grammar point in Section C — students often confuse 'can' (ability) with 'may' (permission) and 'should' (advice) with 'must' (necessity). Use Keller's repeated 'should' to demonstrate hypothetical modal use before moving to the fill-in task. The speaking activity here (public-speaking method) connects directly to Keller's essay: students practise the same grateful-advocacy voice that Keller models. Writing Task (descriptive paragraph, page 37) is not anchored in class; flag it as a homework or follow-up assignment.

items.source_lo: Student reads an informational essay critically, distinguishing true from false statements and sequencing the author's key ideas across a structured plan.
items.item_stem:
Helen Keller, who is blind and deaf, describes what she would most wish to see if given three days of sight. Read the statements below and write TRUE or FALSE for each. For every TRUE statement, write one sentence from the essay that supports it; for every FALSE statement, write a corrected version of the statement.

1. On the first day, Keller would spend time visiting museums to see the history of humankind.
2. Keller believes that her sense of touch gives her access to beauty that intensifies rather than satisfies her longing for sight.
3. On the third day, Keller plans to stand at a busy city corner and observe people going about their lives.
4. Keller concludes that the sense of hearing is the most delightful of all the senses.

options.text: On the first day, Keller would spend time visiting museums to see the history of humankind.
Keller believes that her sense of touch gives her access to beauty that intensifies rather than satisfies her longing for sight.
On the third day, Keller plans to stand at a busy city corner and observe people going about their lives.
Keller concludes that the sense of hearing is the most delightful of all the senses.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: 1. FALSE — On the first day Keller would see the people whose kindness has enriched her life; museum visits are planned for the second day. 2. TRUE — Keller describes how touch reveals delicate symmetry and texture, yet this richness makes her long even more for sight. 3. TRUE — On the third day she imagines standing at a busy city corner merely watching people and reading their faces. 4. FALSE — Keller concludes that sight, not hearing, must be the most delightful sense.

items.question_text:
Helen Keller, who is blind and deaf, describes what she would most wish to see if given three days of sight. Read the statements below and write TRUE or FALSE for each. For every TRUE statement, write one sentence from the essay that supports it; for every FALSE statement, write a corrected version of the statement.

1. On the first day, Keller would spend time visiting museums to see the history of humankind.
2. Keller believes that her sense of touch gives her access to beauty that intensifies rather than satisfies her longing for sight.
3. On the third day, Keller plans to stand at a busy city corner and observe people going about their lives.
4. Keller concludes that the sense of hearing is the most delightful of all the senses.

items.source_lo: Student speaks fluently for one minute on a personal viewpoint, organising ideas using a given prompt framework and maintaining audience awareness.
items.item_stem:
Helen Keller, who experienced the world entirely through touch, smell, and hearing, believed that sight would be the most delightful of all the senses. She urged sighted readers to use their eyes 'as if tomorrow they might be struck blind.'

Speak for one minute on the sense you personally value the most. Organise your response using this framework:
— Name the sense and state why you value it above the others.
— Describe one specific experience in which that sense gave you joy or understanding.
— Reflect: if you were to lose this sense, what would you miss most?
— Close with one piece of advice to a peer about appreciating their senses fully.

Do not read from a script. Maintain eye contact with your audience.

expected_elements: Clearly names the chosen sense and gives a specific personal reason.
Describes a vivid, concrete experience linked to that sense.
Reflection on loss is thoughtful, not merely hypothetical or vague.
Closing advice is directed to the audience and flows naturally from the reflection.

items.question_text:
Helen Keller, who experienced the world entirely through touch, smell, and hearing, believed that sight would be the most delightful of all the senses. She urged sighted readers to use their eyes 'as if tomorrow they might be struck blind.'

Speak for one minute on the sense you personally value the most. Organise your response using this framework:
— Name the sense and state why you value it above the others.
— Describe one specific experience in which that sense gave you joy or understanding.
— Reflect: if you were to lose this sense, what would you miss most?
— Close with one piece of advice to a peer about appreciating their senses fully.

Do not read from a script. Maintain eye contact with your audience.

items.source_lo: Student identifies modal verbs and their functions (possibility, necessity, permission, ability, moral obligation) and uses them correctly in context-specific sentences.
items.item_stem:
Helen Keller uses modal verbs throughout her essay to express what she wishes, plans, and urges others to do. For example, she writes 'I should arise with the dawn' (expressing a wish) and 'You ought to use your eyes as if tomorrow you would be stricken blind' (expressing moral obligation).

Part A — Read each sentence below. Underline the modal verb and write its function from this list: possibility / necessity / ability / polite request / moral obligation.

1. Every person can find beauty in the world if they look carefully enough.
2. We ought to appreciate the gifts of sight and hearing while we have them.
3. It might be difficult to imagine life without any of the five senses.
4. May I have a moment to describe what this sunrise means to me?
5. She must engage with the world fully — through every sense available to her.

Part B — Write your own sentence for each function below, inspired by the theme of Helen Keller's essay (appreciating the senses).

1. A sentence using a modal verb to express possibility.
2. A sentence using a modal verb to express moral obligation.

teacher_guide.suggested_answer: Part A: 1. can — ability; 2. ought to — moral obligation; 3. might — possibility; 4. May — polite request; 5. must — necessity. Part B: Accept any grammatically correct sentences that use an appropriate modal and connect to the theme of appreciating the senses, e.g. 'We might take our hearing for granted until the day we lose it' (possibility); 'We ought to listen to birdsong with full attention every morning' (moral obligation).

items.question_text:
Helen Keller uses modal verbs throughout her essay to express what she wishes, plans, and urges others to do. For example, she writes 'I should arise with the dawn' (expressing a wish) and 'You ought to use your eyes as if tomorrow you would be stricken blind' (expressing moral obligation).

Part A — Read each sentence below. Underline the modal verb and write its function from this list: possibility / necessity / ability / polite request / moral obligation.

1. Every person can find beauty in the world if they look carefully enough.
2. We ought to appreciate the gifts of sight and hearing while we have them.
3. It might be difficult to imagine life without any of the five senses.
4. May I have a moment to describe what this sunrise means to me?
5. She must engage with the world fully — through every sense available to her.

Part B — Write your own sentence for each function below, inspired by the theme of Helen Keller's essay (appreciating the senses).

1. A sentence using a modal verb to express possibility.
2. A sentence using a modal verb to express moral obligation.
